item.title: Shifting Winds: Bangladesh in an Age of Fragmented Interdependence. [ tbsnews ]
Dar arrives in Manila to attend Asean Regional Forum ministerial meeting. [ dawn ]
item.description:
Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar arrived in Manila on Wednesday to participate in the 33rd Asean Regional Forum (ARF) ministerial meeting, the Foreign Office (FO) said.

“A busy programme of engagements lies ahead, including participation in the ARF plenary meeting and bilateral meetings with counterparts on the sidelines,” the FO said in a post on the social media platform X.

It said that upon his arrival, Dar was received by Elmer G Cato, the assistant secretary at the Philippines’ foreign affairs department, Pakistan’s Ambassador to the Philippines Asima Rabbani and other officials.

In a statement issued on Tuesday night, the FO said that the high-level meeting will bring together foreign ministers and delegates from 27 ARF member states, adding that the Asean secretary general will also attend the meeting.

It said that Dar will participate in discussions on “political and security issues affecting the Asia-Pacific region, with a special focus on promoting peace, stability, and mutual cooperation through sustained dialogue and diplomacy”.

He will also hold bilateral meetings with his counterpart from the Philippines and several other participating dignitaries on the sidelines of the meeting, it said.

“Established in 1994, the ARF has remained a pivotal platform for multilateral consultations on regional security issues. Since becoming a member in 2004, Pakistan has maintained an active role within the forum, consistently offering constructive contributions to its regional programmes and objectives,” the FO statement concluded.

Dar meets with Russian, Bangladesh FMs

On the sidelines of the Asean meeting, Dar met with Bangladesh FM Khalilur Rehman, who is also the president-elect of the 81st session of the United Nations General Assembly, the FO said.

The ministers “reviewed the positive trajectory of Pakistan–Bangladesh relations and the renewed impetus by the deputy prime minister’s visit to Bangladesh last year”, the statement said, adding they reaffirmed their “shared commitment to further strengthening bilateral ties”.

The FO further said that the two dignitaries welcomed growing engagement between their countries, including “enhanced people-to-people contacts and improved connectivity following the commencement of direct flights this year”.

They agreed to sustain the “positive trajectory”, the statement added. Dar and Rehman also exchanged views on “regional and international issues of mutual interest”, the FO said.

In January, direct flights between Pakistan and Bangladesh commenced after a 14-year hiatus as an inaugural flight from Dhaka landed at Karachi Airport.

Ahead of the Asean moot, Dar met with Russian FM Sergey Lavrov in Manila.

The two leaders reviewed bilateral relations between their countries and reaffirmed their commitment to “further strengthening cooperation in trade, energy, connectivity, and other key areas, as well as enhancing coordination at multilateral forums”, the FO said.

“They underscored the importance of sustained high-level engagement in advancing the Pakistan-Russia partnership,” it added.

The two foreign ministers also exchanged views on key regional and international developments and agreed to remain in close contact.

Dar visits ADB headquarters in Manila

On the sidelines of his visit to Manila, Dar also visited the headquarters of the Asian Development Bank (ADB), where he was received by the Director General for the Central and West Asia Department, Leah Gutierrez, the FO said.

Dar signed the ADB Visitors’ Book upon his arrival. The FO said “the interaction provided an opportunity to exchange views on the ADB’s evolving development agenda, its partnership with Pakistan, and the role of Pakistani professionals at the institution”.

According to the FO, the discussions focused on the bank’s development agenda and “the important role being played by Pakistani professionals in advancing the Bank’s work across a broad range of sectors and regions.”

Dar appreciated their contributions to the ADB’s mission and acknowledged the distinction they bring to Pakistan through their leadership and expertise, it added.

He also congratulated Nashita Mohsin on assuming her responsibilities as Pakistan’s executive director to the ADB and wished her success in advancing Pakistan’s engagement with the bank.

item.title: Why Delhi's streets tell a bigger democratic story. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
Politics has never depended solely on votes, parliamentary arithmetic, or ideological conviction. It has always depended on stories that societies tell about themselves.
Every government seeks to define what counts as patriotism, dissent, responsibility, and disorder. Every protest movement attempts to rewrite those definitions.
The confrontation that unfolded on the streets of New Delhi during the "Sangsad Chalo" march was therefore about far more than examination paper leaks or the resignation of a cabinet minister.
It revealed an increasingly visible struggle over who possesses the authority to shape political legitimacy in the world's largest democracy.
The immediate trigger appeared familiar. Allegations of repeated examination irregularities, frustrations over unemployment, and accusations of administrative negligence brought thousands of young people to the capital.
Such grievances are hardly unique to India. Across South Asia, public examinations have become gateways to shrinking opportunities rather than pathways to social mobility.
When those gateways are compromised by corruption or incompetence, the consequences extend well beyond disappointed candidates. Faith in merit itself begins to erode.
Yet what distinguished Delhi's protests was not simply the anger of students. It was the remarkable transformation of language surrounding the movement.
A label that had reportedly been intended as an insult evolved into the name of a movement that many participants embraced with unmistakable irony.
The so-called Cockroach Janata Party became more than an online joke. It illustrated how digital political culture increasingly allows citizens to neutrali z e ridicule by appropriating it.
This reversal deserves closer attention because it represents one of the defining characteristics of contemporary politics.
Throughout much of the 20th century, political elites possessed significant control over public narratives. Governments issued statements, newspapers interpreted them, and opposition parties attempted to respond through speeches or organised demonstrations.
Today, however, political communication travels through an entirely different ecosystem. Every slogan is instantly dissected, remixed, parodied, and redistributed by millions of users before official explanations can even catch up.
The Delhi protests demonstrated this transformation with remarkable clarity. Instead of expending energy denying a derogatory label, many young participants incorporated it into their own political identity.
Humour became an instrument of resistance rather than a distraction from it. Memes circulated alongside protest schedules. Satirical artwork travelled together with eyewitness videos. The internet did not merely report the movement. It actively participated in constructing it.
This phenomenon carries profound implications for democratic politics.
Language has traditionally been one of the most powerful tools available to governments. Describing protesters as irresponsible, violent, misguided, or anti-national often shapes public attitudes before any investigation into underlying grievances begins.
Labels simplify complex realities. They reduce citizens into stereotypes that are easier to dismiss than to engage.
Digital participation has complicated this strategy. Once a politically - charged word enters online circulation, its meaning becomes remarkably unstable. Users reinterpret it through jokes, graphics, music, animation, and collective storytelling.
The original speaker no longer retains exclusive ownership over the expression. Political authority therefore loses one of its oldest advantages, namely the ability to impose definitions upon public debate.
That reality should concern governments far beyond India. The rapid spread of participatory digital culture means that political legitimacy increasingly depends not only upon policy performance but also upon communicative intelligence.
Public officials accustomed to one-way messaging frequently underestimate how quickly their words may be transformed into symbols of resistance.
Nevertheless, the Delhi episode should not be romantici z ed. The willingness of young people to reclaim ridicule demonstrates creativity, but creativity alone cannot resolve structural problems.
Examination paper leaks, educational inequality, unemployment, and institutional mistrust remain deeply rooted challenges requiring administrative competence rather than rhetorical adaptation. A successful meme cannot replace successful governance.
The police response similarly raises uncomfortable questions regarding democratic proportionality.
Governments unquestionably possess the responsibility to maintain public order, particularly near sensitive institutions such as Parliament. Peaceful demonstrations may sometimes deteriorate into confrontations involving damaged property or injured security personnel. States cannot simply abandon their obligation to ensure safety.
At the same time, democratic governments derive legitimacy not merely from maintaining order but from demonstrating restraint while doing so.
The widespread circulation of images depicting tear gas, baton charges, barricades, and injured protesters inevitably shapes public perception regardless of official explanations.
Modern policing unfolds before countless smartphone cameras rather than behind institutional walls. Every tactical decision becomes part of a broader political narrative.
This is precisely where governments encounter a difficult paradox. Strong police action may temporarily disperse crowds, yet visual evidence of that action often amplifies public attention toward the very grievances authorities hoped to contain.
Social media platforms reward emotionally charged content. Images of confrontation generally travel farther than press briefings describing procedural necessity.
Consequently, contemporary protests increasingly operate within two interconnected arenas. One exists physically on streets, university campuses, and public squares. The other exists digitally across platforms where photographs, livestreams, edited videos, and satirical content compete for attention.
Success in one arena does not automatically guarantee success in the other. Governments may restore traffic flow while simultaneously losing control over the public narrative.
The Delhi protests illustrate how these parallel arenas constantly interact.
A barricade dismantled in the street quickly becomes a symbolic image online. A viral slogan encourages more citizens to participate offline. Official statements trigger fresh rounds of digital reinterpretation. Political communication now resembles an endless feedback loop rather than a sequence of isolated events.
There is another dimension that deserves equal scrutiny. The movement reflected growing impatience among younger citizens who increasingly measure democratic performance through lived experience rather than constitutional promise.
Elections remain essential, but they no longer exhaust expectations of accountability. Young voters seek functioning institutions, transparent recruitment processes, credible examinations, and opportunities proportional to their qualifications.
When those expectations repeatedly collapse, frustration naturally extends beyond individual ministries toward broader questions of governance.
This generational shift helps explain why seemingly technical administrative failures often acquire political significance. Examination irregularities are no longer viewed simply as bureaucratic mistakes. They become evidence of a larger system perceived to be indifferent toward aspiration itself.
In societies where educational achievement represents the primary route to economic mobility, compromised examinations undermine the social contract between citizens and the state.
T he symbolism of the Cockroach Janata Party therefore extends beyond satire. It represents an emerging political language in which identity is negotiated collectively rather than imposed from above.
The movement suggests that ridicule has become an increasingly unreliable instrument of political control because audiences no longer consume official narratives passively. They reshape them, often with astonishing speed and creativity.
Yet this transformation should also encourage reflection among protest movements themselves. Digital visibility can generate extraordinary momentum, but momentum is not the same as institutional change.
Hashtags trend for hours. Governments remain for years. Sustaining democratic pressure requires organi z ation, negotiation, policy proposals, and strategic patience alongside symbolic mobili z ation.
Otherwise, movements risk becoming memorable cultural moments without producing meaningful structural reform.
The Indian government likewise faces an important choice. It may interpret these protests simply as another episode of public disorder requiring administrative management.
Alternatively, it may recogni z e them as indicators of a deeper crisis of confidence among younger citizens. The second interpretation demands considerably greater political courage because it requires acknowledging that dissatisfaction cannot always be attributed to opposition parties or external provocation.
The Delhi confrontation should therefore be understood within a broader international pattern. From university campuses to digital platforms, young citizens increasingly refuse to separate cultural expression from political participation.
Humour, irony, music, visual art, and internet culture have become legitimate vehicles for articulating civic demands. Traditional political observers often dismiss these forms as frivolous because they differ from older methods of mobili z ation. Such dismissal misunderstands the communication habits of an entire generation.
Political participation has never remained static. Earlier generations relied upon pamphlets, posters, underground newspapers, trade union meetings, and public rallies. Today's activists possess smartphones capable of broadcasting events worldwide within seconds.
Their political imagination is shaped by interconnected digital communities rather than isolated local organi z ations. Consequently, symbols travel across borders with unprecedented speed. A protest slogan coined in one country may inspire movements thousands of kilometres away within days.
South Asia illustrates this regional interconnectedness particularly well. Young people in Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, Nepal, and Sri Lanka increasingly observe one another's political experiences through social media rather than conventional news alone.
They borrow slogans, adapt protest tactics, reinterpret symbols, and exchange strategies in real time. National politics remains distinct, but political culture has become increasingly transnational.
This interconnectedness creates opportunities as well as risks. Constructive ideas regarding transparency, accountability, and civic participation can spread rapidly across societies. So can misinformation, conspiracy theories, and polari z ation.
Democratic resilience therefore depends not merely upon technological access but upon strengthening critical thinking, institutional credibility, and public trust. Digital literacy has become as important to democracy as constitutional literacy.
HM Nazmul Alam is an Academic, Journalist, and Political Analyst based in Dhaka, Bangladesh. Currently he teaches at IUBAT.

item.title: When growing old means growing invisible. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
While everyone was caught up in the World Cup, this particular story quickly faded from the headlines: The elderly woman found dead in her Mirpur apartment, weeks after she had passed away alone. The real tragedy was not just in her death, but in how she went unnoticed for so long.
Family ties used to be the main source of support in our society, so this kind of invisibility once felt rare. Now, it is happening more often.
A country ageing faster than it admits
Bangladesh often thinks of itself as a young country, but the numbers tell a different story.
Government estimates say about 13 million people, or around 8% of the population, are already over 60. This number is expected to rise above 20 million soon, which will change the country’s health and social care needs in ways that are not yet fully recognized.
At the same time, growing older is now more often linked to long-term illness and dependency, rather than rest and family support. Studies in Bangladesh show that more than half of older adults have several chronic conditions, such as hypertension, diabetes, heart disease, and respiratory problems.
It is a good thing that people are living longer, but without enough support, this becomes a silent crisis.
When the family-based care model starts to weaken
For many years, families in Bangladesh cared for their elderly members. This worked because families were bigger, migration was limited, and everyone lived close enough to help each other every day.
That reality is changing.
Urban growth, migration, and nuclear families are making it harder to care for elders in traditional ways. Adult children often leave for work in other cities or countries, leaving elderly parents behind. Even when families are in the same city, time pressure and economic demands make it difficult to give regular care.
This does not mean families are abandoning their elders on purpose. Instead, there is more distance now -- physically, emotionally, and in daily life.
The tragedy in Mirpur is a clear example of this change, but it is not a rare case.
Bangladesh’s healthcare system is still largely set up for short-term problems, such as treating sudden illnesses or hospital visits. It is not as prepared to handle the long-term needs of ageing, such as ongoing diseases, gradual decline, disability, and end-of-life support.
There is little organized monitoring for elderly people who live alone. Community support is scattered, and families often have to deal with illness without much help or advice from medical professionals.
Research shows that over 90% of older adults in Bangladesh have health problems that need medical care. This often means they go to the hospital many times, which increases costs for their families.
This is why palliative care becomes central.
Many people think palliative care is only for the end of life, but it is more than that. It aims to improve the lives of people with serious or long-term illnesses by managing pain, providing emotional support, and coordinating care.
The World Health Organisation estimates that tens of millions of people worldwide require palliative care each year, yet most in low- and middle-income countries do not receive it.
Bangladesh is part of this gap.
With chronic diseases rising fast and hospitals already full, palliative care is not a luxury in Bangladesh. It is a vital part of the health system that is still missing.
The hidden cost of neglect
When adequate care systems are absent, the burden does not disappear but shifts.
Families are the first to feel the cost. In many homes, women take on most of the caregiving, often cutting back on work or leaving their jobs to care for older relatives.
The healthcare system also feels the strain. Many older patients go to the hospital for problems that could be managed at home or in the community, which contributes to overcrowding and makes already-busy hospitals less efficient.
There is also a human cost that is harder to see. Loneliness in older adults is more often linked to depression, memory problems, and worse physical health. In busy cities, people often notice this isolation only when a crisis happens.
Why invisibility is the real problem
The Mirpur case makes us think about something uncomfortable. It is not just about one person not being found in time. It shows how easily older people can become invisible in today’s cities.
Cities like Dhaka are crowded, but being close to others does not mean older people are seen. Elderly people living alone can go unnoticed for days. In rural areas, migration has created another kind of distance, in which care is expected but not always provided.
Growing old in Bangladesh now often means being physically close to others but receiving insufficient social attention.
What needs to change
The solutions are not new or complicated. What we need is to bring them together, not invent something entirely different.
Palliative care can be added to basic healthcare services. Community health workers can learn the basics of caring for older people. Local groups can find elderly people living alone and check on them regularly. Home-based care, run by public, private, and NGO partners, can offer ongoing support when hospitals cannot.
Just as important, caregiving should be seen as a responsibility for everyone, not just something families handle quietly on their own.
Other countries that have adopted these systems have seen improved quality of life, fewer hospital visits, and greater dignity at the end of life.
The woman who died alone in Mirpur should not be just another sad news story. Her experience shows a bigger change that is already happening.
Bangladesh is ageing and the question is whether growing old in this country will ever mean care, dignity, and being seen, or quietly fading away.
Shafiq R Bhuiyan writes on how communication, culture and corporate social responsibility (CSR) converge to shape a more conscious and compassionate society.

item.title: Protection is a life skill. Why aren’t we teaching it?. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
A few weeks ago, I had the opportunity to facilitate a Young Women's Protection Leadership Bootcamp that brought together 22 young women from different parts of Bangladesh and neighboring countries. Over several days, we discussed leadership, advocacy, protection risks, and the challenges young women face in their communities.
I expected to learn a lot from the participants. What I did not expect was how often I would hear the same sentence: "This happens in my community too."
As we discussed real-life cases from Kurigram, Naogaon, Cox's Bazar, Bhubaneswar in India, Nepal, and elsewhere, the details changed but the stories felt remarkably familiar.
A girl leaving school because she was married off too early. A young woman excluded from community decision-making. A survivor struggling to access support services. A family considering a dangerous migration journey because poverty leaves them with few options. A young activist facing online abuse for speaking out about women's rights.
Violence against women and girls remains one of the most widely reported protection concerns in Bangladesh.
Different places. Different circumstances. Yet the same patterns kept appearing.
What struck me most was that these situations rarely emerged overnight. The warning signs were often visible long before the harm occurred.
Yet many individuals, families, and communities did not recognize them as protection concerns until the consequences became severe.
That realization stayed with me long after the bootcamp ended.
Why do we teach children mathematics, science, and language from an early age, but rarely teach them about protection?
By protection education, I do not mean specialized humanitarian training. I mean helping children and young people understand personal safety, rights, consent, gender equality, online risks, discrimination, help-seeking pathways, and how to recognize and respond to abuse, exploitation, and violence.
These are not advanced concepts reserved for lawyers, social workers, or humanitarian professionals. They are life skills.
Yet many young people grow up without ever learning them.
The consequences are all around us. Bangladesh has made remarkable progress in education, health, and poverty reduction. Yet many protection challenges continue to affect the lives of women and girls.
According to Unicef, Bangladesh still has the highest child marriage rate in Asia, with 51% of women aged 20-24 married before the age of 18.
At the same time, violence against women remains widespread. UN Women reports that nearly three out of four ever-married women in Bangladesh have experienced some form of intimate partner violence in their lifetime.
These figures remind us that legal reforms and economic progress alone are not enough. Prevention must begin much earlier, through awareness, education, and changes in social norms.
Many of these risks are rooted in social norms that children absorb from an early age. In many communities, boys are encouraged to be confident, independent, and outspoken. Girls are often expected to be careful, obedient, and mindful of family reputation. While boys are encouraged to explore the world, girls are often taught to navigate its limits.
These expectations may seem small at first. Over time, however, they influence opportunities, choices, and power. For many girls, this can mean fewer opportunities to continue education, participate in leadership, access services, or make decisions about their own lives. It can also increase their vulnerability to violence, exploitation, and discrimination.
This is precisely why protection education matters.
Protection education cannot eliminate every risk. But it can help people recognize risks before they become crises. It can teach young people how to identify unsafe situations, seek support, understand their rights, challenge harmful norms, stay safe online, and support others facing violence or discrimination.
Most importantly, it can help young people see themselves as part of the solution.
I witnessed this firsthand during the bootcamp. The young women participating were not waiting for someone else to solve the problems affecting their communities. They were analyzing protection concerns, mapping risks, identifying barriers, and developing practical action plans.
They discussed child marriage, trafficking, gender-based violence, climate-related risks, and social exclusion with remarkable insight and determination.
Many had experienced some of these challenges personally. Others had witnessed them in their communities. Yet they were not defined by those experiences. They were transforming experience into leadership.
Too often, young women are viewed primarily through the lens of vulnerability. We describe them as victims, beneficiaries, or people in need of support. While support remains essential, this perspective overlooks something equally important.
Young women are leaders. They are advocates. They are changemakers. When given knowledge, space, and opportunity, they do not simply protect themselves. They help protect others, strengthen community resilience, and contribute to safer and more inclusive societies.
This is why programs that invest in young women's leadership are so valuable. They build confidence, strengthen networks, and create local leaders who can identify risks and drive solutions. However, these opportunities should not be limited to those fortunate enough to participate in specialized workshops.
Protection education should become part of how we educate and prepare all young people for life. Protection education does not require a new subject in the curriculum. It can be integrated into existing lessons through discussions on rights, gender equality, digital safety, consent, safe migration, bullying prevention, and help-seeking mechanisms.
Schools, families, community organizations, and youth groups all have a role to play. We teach children how to cross a road safely because we want to prevent harm before it happens. Teaching them how to recognize violence, seek help, understand their rights, and protect themselves and others deserves the same priority.
P rotection is not only a humanitarian issue. It is a life skill. And every child should have the opportunity to learn it.
Fariha Hossain is working on humanitarian and refugee issues.

item.title: Helping our footwear industry fulfil its potential. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
It is no secret that Bangladesh's non-leather footwear industry has the potential, as well as the favourable conditions, to be one of the country's great export success stories.
Yet, at a time when global brands are actively diversifying their supply chains and facilitating emerging manufacturing hubs, it is unfortunate that Bangladesh has failed to capitalize on the opportunity, with export growth slowing to just 1.6% in the last fiscal year.
The reasons behind this disappointing performance are both familiar and frustrating: High borrowing costs, limited access to bank financing, rising production expenses, unreliable utility services, delays in importing raw materials, and lingering political uncertainty have combined to stifle investment and expansion.
While manufacturers report that demand exists, many factories are already operating at full capacity and cannot expand because financing has become increasingly difficult to obtain.
This is particularly concerning because Bangladesh possesses many of the ingredients needed to become a globally competitive footwear exporter. The country has a sizable workforce, years of manufacturing experience, and growing recognition among international buyers.
What is lacking, however, is an enabling business environment that allows enterprises to scale up confidently and compete with regional rivals that have captured a far larger share of the global footwear market.
The government must, therefore, treat the non-leather footwear industry as a strategic export sector. Improving access to affordable finance, ensuring uninterrupted gas and electricity supplies, streamlining customs procedures, and reducing unnecessary regulatory bottlenecks should become immediate priorities.
At the same time, policy-makers must provide a stable and predictable investment climate that inspires confidence among both domestic manufacturers and foreign buyers.
With the right policies and timely reforms, the non-leather footwear sector could generate billions in export earnings, create thousands of quality jobs, and help diversify the country's export basket beyond garments.
The window of opportunity is open, but not indefinitely -- and Bangladesh cannot afford to let another global opportunity slip away.

item.title: Getting past vengeance. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The recent reported bulldozer attack and arson on the home of an Awami League leader in Patuakhali represent a deeply disturbing episode that has no place in a society governed by the rule of law.
The broader issue extends far beyond a single attack; it speaks to an increasingly dangerous tendency to take matters into one's own hands, disregarding due process and replacing justice with intimidation and destruction.
No grievance, allegation, or disagreement can ever justify the use of violence against people or property. Such actions erode public confidence in the institutions responsible for upholding the law, and encourage a culture where force is perceived as a legitimate means of settling disputes.
If left unchecked, this mindset risks becoming normalized, creating an environment where similar incidents become more frequent and increasingly severe.
It is therefore imperative that the authorities conduct a swift, impartial, and transparent investigation into the attack, identify everyone responsible, and ensure that they are held accountable in accordance with the law.
Justice must be ensured in this matter, reinforcing the principle that no individual or group is above the law.
At the same time, Bangladesh must address the mentality that fuels such incidents. Respect for legal processes, tolerance of differing views, and the peaceful resolution of conflicts are essential pillars of any democratic society.
Public awareness, stronger law enforcement, and consistent accountability all have a role to play in reversing the growing acceptance of mob behaviour and vigilante actions.
Violence cannot be allowed to become an accepted language of disagreement. This attack should serve as a wake-up call for both the authorities and society at large. Such acts must be unequivocally rejected, and every effort must be made to ensure they are never repeated.

item.title: AI can power Bangladesh's next growth model. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
Bangladesh is entering a decisive phase in its development. As it prepares for life beyond its least developed country (LDC) status, it faces two defining challenges of the 21st century. The first is sustaining economic growth in an increasingly competitive world. The second is successfully transitioning to an upper-middle-income economy.
To achieve these goals, Bangladesh cannot rely on its traditional old low-cost growth model. Instead, it needs a new productivity-driven model built on artificial intelligence (AI), human capital, agriculture, small and medium enterprises (SMEs), and IT-enabled services (ITES).
Building the next growth model
While LDC graduation is assessed through specific metrics - gross national income (GNI) per capita, the human assets index (HAI), and the economic and environmental vulnerability index (EVI), attaining upper-middle-income status depends on sustained productivity growth, industrial upgrading, technological innovation, and a highly skilled workforce.
New technologies are transforming agriculture, industry, and services by boosting productivity, reshaping business models, and changing the nature of work.
Bangladesh's success in both its economic transition and technological transformation will therefore depend on how effectively it harnesses these technologies to enhance productivity, competitiveness, and innovation across all sectors.
For decades, the ready-made garments (RMG) sector, built on a labour-intensive manufacturing framework, has been the primary engine of growth. While this strategy delivered remarkable economic progress, it is increasingly vulnerable to automation and global competition.
Rather than attempting an abrupt leap to fully automated smart factories, Bangladesh should adopt a pragmatic, AI-enabled growth strategy centered on three foundational pillars: Agriculture, services, and SMEs.
Equally important is sustained investment in human capital through continuous reskilling and upskilling, supported by an education system that equips graduates with disciplinary expertise, digital competence, AI literacy, and interdisciplinary skills. Together, these priorities can underpin sustainable productivity growth and long-term competitiveness.
Agriculture: Raising productivity through AI
Agriculture remains a cornerstone of Bangladesh's economy, employing the lion’s share of the labour force, contributing approximately 11-12% of GDP, ensuring national food security, supplying raw materials for agro-processing industries, and generating valuable export earnings.
Yet many farmers continue to struggle with low productivity, climate risks, volatile markets, and limited access to timely information, leaving them vulnerable to intermediaries and fluctuating prices.
AI can transform this landscape through precision agriculture. AI-enabled digital platforms can provide real-time crop disease diagnosis, weather and pest alerts, and recommendations on irrigation and fertilizer use, helping farmers reduce costs, improve yields, and strengthen climate resilience without requiring expensive machinery.
AI can also forecast market demand and price trends, enabling farmers to make better production and marketing decisions while reducing post-harvest losses.
Technology alone, however, is not enough. AI tools must be affordable, user-friendly, and accessible through Bangla-language, voice-enabled interfaces, supported by agricultural extension services, cooperatives, NGOs, and agribusinesses. Equipping extension workers with digital and AI skills will be essential to ensuring that smallholder and less-literate farmers also benefit from this transformation.
SMEs and ITES: Expanding high-value growth
The cottage, micro, small, and medium enterprise (CMSME) sector is the lifeblood of Bangladesh's economy, accounting for about 99% of all enterprises and nearly 80% of industrial employment. However, systemic barriers in accessing finance and adopting technology often stall their growth.
AI can level the playing field by automating administrative tasks, optimizing inventory, improving fraud detection, and streamlining credit assessments, making small businesses more creditworthy for formal financial institutions.
AI-driven demand forecasting and supply chain management can also help agro-processing and manufacturing SMEs connect more effectively with domestic and international markets.
Alongside conventional SMEs, ITES represent a frontier opportunity. Compared with heavy manufacturing, the sector requires relatively modest capital investment but yields high-value returns in software development, business process outsourcing (BPO), and cloud solutions. As global demand for digital services continues to expand, ITES can generate high-value employment for educated youth and diversify Bangladesh's export base beyond apparel.
The services sector will also play a central role in Bangladesh's next phase of growth. AI can augment human capabilities, enabling institutions to deliver more accessible, affordable, and efficient services.
In healthcare, AI-assisted diagnostic tools can support clinicians, particularly in underserved areas where specialists are scarce. In education, AI-powered personalized learning platforms can help improve learning outcomes and expand access to quality instruction. In financial services, AI can strengthen credit assessment, streamline microfinance operations, detect fraud, and extend banking services to underserved communities.
The policy imperative
First , Bangladesh must make digital literacy and AI literacy national priorities. While government initiatives are already equipping young people with advanced digital skills, comparable efforts are needed at the grassroots so that farmers, SME owners, and workers can confidently adopt AI-enabled technologies.
Second , policy-makers should consider creating regulatory sandboxes and targeted incentives that encourage AI start-ups to develop solutions tailored to Bangladesh's development challenges. Continued investment in digital infrastructure, secure data ecosystems, and reliable connectivity will provide the foundation for AI innovation to flourish.
Bangladesh’s economic resilience has always rested on the ingenuity and adaptability of its people. By strategically integrating AI into agriculture, SMEs, and services, the country can break free from the low-wage trap.
Lasting prosperity will not belong to the nations with the cheapest labour, but to those that deploy technology most intelligently. Bangladesh must seize this moment to build its next-generation economy.
Dr MM Shahidul Hassan is Distinguished Professor, Eastern University and Former Vice-Chancellor, East West University.

item.title: On the path to economic democracy?. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
Political democracy without economic democracy is meaningless. If people remain trapped in poverty and deprivation, political rights lose their value. True democracy requires the elimination of economic exploitation and inequality, ensuring that citizens can live free from want.
Yet, in Bangladesh, discussions of democracy have long ignored the economic dimension. Recently, however, the new BNP government has begun to include economic democracy in its planning.
Bangladesh’s political and economic foundation lies in its middle and lower-middle classes. Over the past 55 years, the absence of economic democracy has weakened these groups, turning them into a declining class. As a result, political democracy has repeatedly stumbled, and politics itself has fallen under the grip of corruption and criminalization.
Political democracy becomes meaningful only when people’s basic needs are secured. Without solving fundamental economic problems, political freedom remains an empty aspiration.
As Professor Harold Laski observed, until economic equality is established, a truly democratic society cannot exist. In Bangladesh, while political democracy has been debated, economic democracy has been neglected -- causing democracy to falter time and again.
The link between economy and democracy has long existed and for Bangladesh, it is a harsh reality. Experts argue that economic freedom and equality are essential for political stability and democratic progress. History shows that when the economy weakens, the middle class collapses, and democracy cannot survive.
A weakening of the middle class means a weakening of democracy. Politics and economics are like two parallel railway tracks -- without both, the train of democracy cannot move forward. Or it can be compared to a bicycle: If its two wheels do not rotate evenly, it loses balance and falls. When the economy crumbles, politics degenerates, the middle class suffers, and democracy loses its foundation.
Economic democracy also influences other dimensions of democracy. It ensures equal participation for all citizens in collective activity. Yet, in Bangladesh, the richest 10% control 60% of the nation’s wealth, while the bottom 50% -- half the population -- share only 4%. Similarly, the poorest 40% receive just 10% of national income, while the richest 10% take 50%.
Equality, good governance, accountability, and a democratic environment strengthen the economy. A well-organized market builds investor confidence. But when the majority’s economic base erodes, their income and purchasing power decline, weakening democracy itself.
Bangladesh’s history shows that whenever the economy has collapsed, democracy has suffered, and politics has been corrupted. The same pattern is visible globally -- for instance, the Great Depression of the 1930s paved the way for World War II.
During the last 16 years of Awami League rule, corruption was institutionalized under the guise of development, creating an oligarchic authoritarian system. Entrepreneurs were forced to serve ruling party interests, banks were looted, and false statistics were presented as truth.
Honest business became nearly impossible without political connections. As a result, economic inequality doubled compared to 1971. This deadlock was broken in July 2024, when students and citizens rose in an unprecedented mass movement. That uprising echoed the unfinished goals of the 1971 Liberation War -- equality, democracy, justice, and human dignity -- marking the beginning of a new dream for Bangladesh.
In the aftermath, economic issues gained special importance in discussions about building a new Bangladesh. The interim government formed reform commissions and sought consensus through a National Council, but failed to prioritize economic reform.
False statistics about LDC graduation were maintained to serve bureaucratic interests, discouraging investment and stalling job creation. Poverty and inequality grew, contradicting the spirit of the July Revolution. Bangladesh, labeled a so-called “middle-income country,” quickly lost markets and investment opportunities.
The term democracy itself comes from the Greek demos (people) and kratos (rule), emphasizing participation. Economic democracy extends this principle, advocating for ownership, control, and decision-making power to be shared.
Bangladesh has significant potential for democratizing its economy. To achieve this, several fundamental measures are essential but at the heart of it, equitable economic policies must prioritize the poor and marginalized.
Bangladesh’s journey toward being a better democracy cannot succeed without economic democratization. Only by ensuring equality, justice, and shared prosperity can the nation fulfill the unfinished promises of 1971 and the aspirations of the July 2024 Revolution.
Over the past five and a half decades, Bangladesh’s official narrative of economic growth has been bright -- but its dark shadow has been equally long.
Recent private statistics show that the country's Gini coefficient has climbed from 0.30 in 1971 to 0.60, a stark indicator of extreme income inequality. The divide between urban and rural areas, the capital and the periphery, and the rich and the poor is deepening.
Economists argue that, in terms of inequality, today’s Bangladesh resembles the old divide between East and West Pakistan -- two realities within one nation.
Regional gaps and income concentration reinforce this troubling parallel. Rising inequality, uneven regional development, and the shrinking middle class remind us of the pre-independence divide.
While Dhaka enjoys development, opportunity, and power, the rest of the country suffers neglect and frustration. Without economic democracy -- fair distribution of resources, decentralization, and citizen participation -- political democracy cannot be strong or sustainable. Economic injustice undermines democratic justice.
Numbers behind the divide
Official statistics show average GDP growth of 6.5% between 2006 and 2024, with per capita income rising to $2,850. Yet, 40% of the population still lives below the poverty line, earning less than $2 per day. Urban poverty stands at 34%, while rural poverty exceeds 46%.
Revenue collection has fallen from 12% of GDP (2007-08) to just 6.5% (2025-26). Major infrastructure projects -- Padma Bridge, Jamuna Bridge, the metro rail, airports, and the Karnaphuli Tunnel -- are concentrated around Dhaka and nearby regions, intensifying regional inequality.
Dhaka and Chittagong dominate the economy, controlling 75% of industry, trade, energy use, bank loans, and GDP. This concentration mirrors the pre-1971 imbalance, when West Pakistan monopolized resources while East Pakistan was marginalized.
Democratic threat
Inequality is not only about income -- it extends to opportunities, education, healthcare, political influence, and social security. Such disparity weakens democracy, restricts political participation, fuels unrest, and wastes human potential. The anger of marginalized communities grows, threatening national unity.
Bangladesh fought a Liberation War in 1971 against West Pakistan’s economic exploitation, sacrificing countless lives. Yet, development, jobs, investment, education, and healthcare have been centralized in Dhaka, leaving other regions behind. This imbalance undermines democracy and fuels instability.
Globally, after the 2007-08 financial crisis, the idea of economic democracy gained traction as an alternative to capitalist concentration of wealth and corporate monopoly.
Its principles include:
Transparency and accountability -- ensuring institutions and governments remain answerable.
Models such as worker cooperatives, employee ownership funds, community land trusts, and participatory representation demonstrate how economic democracy empowers farmers and workers, boosts productivity, and enhances equality.
Analysts believe Bangladesh can democratize its economy through decentralization -- political, administrative, and fiscal. Empowering local governments would increase citizen participation in economic decisions.
Challenges remain such as a large population, weak institutions, corruption, and lack of capacity. Yet, economic democracy is not just theory -- it is essential for building a just society.
Its implementation would bring Bangladesh closer to achieving constitutional goals of social, political, and economic justice.
Salahuddin Ahmaad Bablu is a senior journalist.

item.title: New Bangladesh government’s rights vow tested by disappearance. [ asiatimes ]
item.description:
